concepts.title: Conduction
concepts.conceptBody:
Conduction

  • Heat is transferred in conduction by collision with neighboring particles within the body.
  • When one end is placed at a higher temperature, atoms or molecules get heat energy and start oscillating with greater amplitude and it collides with neighboring molecules and shares its KE. And in the process heat is transferred from one end to the other end.
  • Average position of atoms/molecules do not change. (No actual transfers of mass is involved).
  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ In steady state, the heat crossing per unit time through any cross-section is constant. Which is given by,

$\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}=-\frac{\text{KA}\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)}{\text{l}}$

where $\text{K}$ = thermal conductivity or conduction coefficient

Note : 

(1) Area of cross-section should be taken perpendicular to direction of the heat flow.

(2) Length 1 should be measured along the direction of heat flow.

(3) In the steady state, the temperature of each cross section remains constant, (though it is not equal to all cross-section). 

Limitations of $\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{Q}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}=\frac{\mathbf{KA}\left( {{\mathbf{T}}_{1}}-{{\mathbf{T}}_{2}} \right)}{\text{l}}$ formula

(i) To be used in the steady state only

(ii) Not applicable when area is not constant

(iii) Not applicable when $\text{K}\ne $ constant

However, in case of variable conditions, (e.g. when $\text{K}$ is a function of $\text{x}$ ) we may use :

$\frac{\text{d}Q}{\text{dT}}=-\text{KA}\frac{\text{dT}}{\text{dx}}$

concepts.title: Electrical Analogy of Thermal Conduction
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrical Analogy of Thermal Conduction

$\text{i}=\left( \frac{{{\text{V}}_{1}}-{{\text{V}}_{2}}}{\text{R}} \right)$

In heat conduction we have $\left( \frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}} \right)=\text{i}=\frac{\left( {{\text{T}}_{1}}-{{\text{T}}_{2}} \right)}{\left( \text{d}/\text{KA} \right)}$

Potential difference $\Leftrightarrow $ Temp. difference

Resistance $\Leftrightarrow $ Thermal resistance $=\left( \frac{\text{d}}{\text{KA}} \right)$

concepts.title: Series Combination of Conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
Series Combination of Conductors

(i) Thermal Resistance 

$\left( \text{Req} \right)={{\text{R}}_{1}}+{{\text{R}}_{2}}$

Derivation :

$\begin{array}{l} \left( {{\text{T}}_{1}}-\text{T} \right)=\text{i}{{R}_{1}} \\ \left( \text{ T}-{{\text{T}}_{2}} \right)=i{{\text{R}}_{2}} \\ \left( {{\text{T}}_{1}}-{{\text{T}}_{2}} \right)=\text{i}\left( {{\text{R}}_{1}}+{{\text{R}}_{2}} \right)=\text{i}\left( {{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ Req}=\left( {{\text{R}}_{1}}+{{\text{R}}_{2}} \right)$

$\left[ {{\text{R}}_{1}}=\frac{{{\text{l}}_{1}}}{{{\text{K}}_{1}}\text{ A}},\left( {{\text{R}}_{2}}=\frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{ A}} \right) \right]$

(ii) Equivalent Thermal Conductivity 

$\begin{array}{l} {{l}_{\text{eq }}}={{l}_{1}}+{{l}_{2}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\left( {{l}_{1}}+{{l}_{2}} \right)}{\text{KA}}=\frac{{{l}_{1}}}{{{\text{K}}_{1}}\text{ A}}+\frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{ A}} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ K}=\frac{{{\text{l}}_{1}}+{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{\left( \frac{{{\text{l}}_{1}}}{{{\text{K}}_{1}}}+\frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{{{\text{K}}_{2}}} \right)}$

$\therefore {{\text{K}}_{\text{eq}}}=\frac{\mathop{\sum }_{\text{i}=1}^{\text{n}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{i}}}}{\mathop{\sum }_{\text{i}=1}^{\text{n}}\frac{{{\text{l}}_{\text{i}}}}{{{\text{k}}_{\text{i}}}}}=\frac{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\text{dx}}{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{K}}}$

concepts.title: Parallel Combination of Conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
Parallel Combination of Conductors

(i) Equivalent Thermal resistance 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{R}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{l}}{{{\text{K}}_{1}}{{\text{A}}_{1}}},{{\text{R}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{l}}{{{\text{K}}_{2}}{{\text{A}}_{2}}} \\ \therefore  \text{i}=\left( {{\text{i}}_{1}}+{{\text{i}}_{2}} \right)\equiv \text{ Net }\text{ heat }\text{ flow }\text{ / }\text{ (time) } \\ \Rightarrow  \frac{\left( {{\text{T}}_{1}}-{{\text{T}}_{2}} \right)}{{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}}}=\frac{\left( {{\text{T}}_{1}}-{{\text{T}}_{2}} \right)}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}+\frac{\left( {{\text{T}}_{1}}-{{\text{T}}_{2}} \right)}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \\ \therefore  \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \end{array}$

     

(ii) Equivalent Thermal Conductivity 

$\frac{\mathrm{KA}}{\mathrm{l}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{~A}_1}{\mathrm{l}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{~A}_2}{\mathrm{l}}$

$\therefore \mathrm{Keq}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{~A}_1+\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{~A}_2}{\mathrm{~A}_1+\mathrm{A}_2}\right)=\frac{\sum \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\sum \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{i}}}=\frac{\int \mathrm{KdA}}{\int \mathrm{dA}}$

concepts.title: Temperature at Junction
concepts.conceptBody:
Temperature of Junction 

To find the temp. of junction, first find the current i, and then use the resistance of the part upto which temperature is to be calculated. 
e.g. ${{\text{T}}_{1}}-\text{T'}=\text{i}{{\text{R}}_{1}}$ 
$\text{T'}={{\text{T}}_{1}}-\text{i}{{\text{R}}_{1}}$


$={{\text{T}}_{1}}-\frac{\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right){{\text{R}}_{1}}}{\left( \frac{{{\text{l}}_{1}}}{{{\text{K}}_{1}}\text{ A}}+\frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{{{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{ A}} \right)}$

examples.title: The length of the two rods made up of the same metal and having the same area of cross-section are $0.6 \mathrm{~m}$ and $0.8 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively. The temperature between the ends of first rod is $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and that for the other rod is 150 and $110^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. For which rod the rate of conduction will be greater
The ratio of thermal conductivity of two rods of different material is $5: 4$. The two rods of same area of cross-section and same thermal resistance will have the lengths in the ratio

Two rods $A$ and $B$ are of equal lengths. Their ends are kept between the same temperature and their area of cross-sections are $A_1$ and $A_2$ and thermal conductivities $K_1$ and $K_2$. The rate of heat transmission in the two rods will be equal, if

Two identical plates of different metals are joined to form a single plate whose thickness is double the thickness of each plate. If the coefficients of conductivity of each plate are 2 and 3 respectively, then the conductivity of composite plate will be

One end of a metal rod of length $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ and area of cross section $100 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ is maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the other end of the rod is maintained at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the quantity of heat transmitted through the rod per minute is (Coefficient of thermal conductivity of material of rod $=100 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}-\mathrm{K}$ )

Two metal cubes $A$ and $B$ of same size are arranged as shown in the figure. The extreme ends of the combination are maintained at the indicated temperatures. The arrangement is thermally insulated. The coefficients of thermal conductivity of $A$ and $B$ are $300 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $200 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. After steady state is reached, the temperature of the interface will be
